title: Colic and gas in babies
excerpt: Babies cry, pull up their legs, and pass gas, and parents panic. Ayurveda's children's text treats this as a tender, temporary phase of infant digestion, and offers gentle, warming care.
body_html:
why the tummy hurts in the early months

If your baby suddenly cries hard in the evening, draws the knees up to the belly, clenches the little fists, and seems better for a moment after passing wind, you are watching one of the most common scenes of early parenthood. It feels alarming, but in most healthy babies it is a passing phase of a digestive system that is still learning its work. Ayurveda has a word for this cramping wind in the belly: it falls under udara shula, plainly meaning pain in the abdomen, driven by vata, the bodily air that governs movement and gas.

Ayurveda's dedicated children's text, the Kashyapa Samhita, treats the infant as a being whose digestive fire and tissues are delicate and still forming, so the care of children is its own branch of medicine, kaumarabhritya (Kashyapa Samhita, Sutrasthana). In a tiny baby, the agni, the digestive capacity, is gentle and easily disturbed by overfeeding, by swallowed air, or by a mother's own diet when nursing. When digestion stumbles, vata builds up as trapped wind, and that is the colic you see. Sushruta similarly counts vata as the dosha most given to movement, distension, and gripping pain (Sushruta Samhita, Sutrasthana), which is exactly how a gassy belly behaves.

meet cold, dry, and erratic with warm, oily, and steady

the gentle traditions: warmth, oil, and rhythm

The classical instinct for a vata problem is simple: meet cold, dry, and erratic with warm, oily, and steady. The Kashyapa Samhita places great weight on the loving, calm handling of the infant and on warm oil care of the small body, and oil massage, abhyanga, is woven through the daily routine of a child (Kashyapa Samhita, Sutrasthana). A warm, unhurried tummy rub does two honest things: it soothes a distressed baby and it helps trapped wind move along.

A second old household tradition is hingu, asafoetida, long valued as a carminative that settles wind and eases cramping (Bhavaprakasha, Nighantu section, on the qualities of hingu). In many Indian homes a tiny smear of hing paste is warmed and applied around the navel from the outside, never fed to a small infant. The third pillar is rhythm. Because the infant's agni is delicate, regular, unhurried feeds, keeping the baby upright a while after, and a calm feeding environment matter more than any remedy. A nursing mother eating warm, simple, easy-to-digest food rather than cold, heavy, or very gas-forming meals also helps, since what she eats reaches the milk.

gentle everyday care

None of this needs to be elaborate. Warmth, a slow belly rub, an upright cuddle to bring up wind, and a steady feeding rhythm cover most ordinary evening fussiness. Keep the room calm, keep your own hands warm, and remember that for most babies this settles on its own by the third or fourth month as the gut matures.

when to see a doctor

This is general information rooted in classical Ayurveda, not a diagnosis or a substitute for medical care. A baby's belly can sometimes signal something more serious, so see a pediatrician or a qualified vaidya promptly if your baby has a high-pitched or inconsolable cry that will not settle, fever, vomiting (especially green or forceful vomiting), blood in the stool, a swollen or hard belly, refusal to feed, fewer wet nappies, or poor weight gain. Never put hing, oils, or any preparation in a baby's mouth, and never give honey to a child under one year. When in doubt, get the baby checked: with infants, caution is always the wiser path.

Try This Today

  • warm tummy massage: With clean, warm hands and a little warm oil, rub your baby's belly slowly in gentle clockwise circles around the navel, then bicycle the legs softly. Do it when the baby is calm, not right after a feed.
  • upright after feeds: Hold your baby upright against your shoulder for ten to fifteen minutes after each feed so swallowed air can come up. A gentle pat on the back helps the wind escape.
  • hing paste, outside only: Following the old household tradition, warm a tiny smear of hing (asafoetida) paste and apply it on the skin around the navel, never in the mouth. Keep it light and watch for any redness.
  • calm the feeding scene: Feed in a quiet, unhurried setting with the baby well-latched, so less air is swallowed. A steady daily rhythm settles a delicate infant agni better than any single remedy.

metafields.dek: Babies cry, pull up their legs, and pass gas, and parents panic. Ayurveda's children's text treats this as a tender, temporary phase of infant digestion, and offers gentle, warming care.
metafields.disclaimer: Ayurveda guidance on AyurHQ is supportive care, not emergency or diagnostic medical treatment.
at_a_glance: Infant colic is usually trapped vata (wind) in a still-maturing gut, a passing phase that settles as the baby grows.
Classical care is warmth, gentle tummy oiling (abhyanga), external hing paste around the navel, and a calm, regular feeding rhythm.
Red flags like fever, forceful or green vomiting, blood in stool, or a hard, swollen belly mean see a doctor at once.

glossary_terms.definition: The bodily air, the principle of movement, which governs wind and gas in the belly.
The digestive fire or capacity; in an infant it is gentle and easily disturbed.
Pain in the abdomen, the classical term for cramping belly ache.
The branch of Ayurveda dealing with the care of infants and children.
Oil massage, a daily warming care for the body, central to infant care.
Asafoetida, valued as a carminative that settles wind and cramping.

sources: Kashyapa Samhita, Sutrasthana (kaumarabhritya, infant care and abhyanga)
Sushruta Samhita, Sutrasthana (qualities of vata: distension and gripping pain)
Bhavaprakasha, Nighantu section (qualities of hingu, asafoetida)

faqs.question: What is the meaning of "vata" in Ayurveda?
faqs.answer: In the Ayurvedic tradition, vata is defined as: The bodily air, the principle of movement, which governs wind and gas in the belly.

faqs.question: How can I start addressing this issue today?
faqs.answer: One of the most recommended practices is to warm tummy massage. With clean, warm hands and a little warm oil, rub your baby's belly slowly in gentle clockwise circles around the navel, then bicycle the legs softly. Do it when the baby is calm, not right after a feed.

faqs.question: Are there any specific lifestyle habits I should adopt?
faqs.answer: A key suggestion is to upright after feeds. Hold your baby upright against your shoulder for ten to fifteen minutes after each feed so swallowed air can come up. A gentle pat on the back helps the wind escape.
